title: How to Use Intuition When Evaluating Spiritual Experiences
meta_description: Learn to trust your inner knowing when spiritual experiences feel real but uncertain. Practical guidance for grounding your intuition.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Evaluating Spiritual Experiences
og_description: Learn to trust your inner knowing when spiritual experiences feel real but uncertain. Practical guidance for grounding your intuition.
body:
You Had an Experience That Changed You. Now What?

Something happened. Maybe you felt a presence in the room when no one was there. Maybe you had a dream that came true the next day. Maybe you were driving and suddenly knew you had to turn left instead of right, and that decision kept you out of a five-car pileup. Or maybe it was quieter than that - a knowing that settled into your bones during meditation, a sense that someone you loved was about to reach out, and then your phone buzzed with their name.

Whatever it was, it shook you. Not in a bad way, not exactly. But it shook you because it didn't fit the story you've been told your whole life about what's real and what isn't.

So now you're sitting with it. Turning it over in your mind like a stone you found on a beach. Was that real? Was I just imagining things? Am I losing my grip? Or - and this is the scary one - did something actually happen that I don't have words for?

Here's the thing nobody tells you about spiritual experiences: they don't come with a user manual. You don't get a certificate of authenticity. There's no spiritual Better Business Bureau you can call to check if that vision was legit or just your brain playing tricks on you.

So how do you figure out what's real? How do you trust yourself when everything you've been taught says you shouldn't?

You use your intuition. The very thing that's probably been screaming at you your whole life. The thing you've been taught to ignore.

"The spiritual experience itself isn't the problem. The problem is you've never been taught how to tell the difference between a genuine opening and your own baggage wearing a Halloween costume."

Why Your Brain Is the Worst Judge of Spiritual Experiences

Your brain is a pattern-matching machine built for survival, not truth. It's designed to keep you alive on the savanna, not to discern whether that feeling of being watched was your grandmother's spirit or your anxiety about being alone.

When you have a spiritual experience, your brain immediately does two things. First, it tries to explain it away using whatever approach it has. If you're a skeptic, it says "that was a coincidence" or "you imagined it." If you're a believer, it says "this confirms everything I already thought." Either way, your brain is protecting its existing model of reality. It's not interested in the truth. It's interested in consistency.

Second, your brain scans for threat. Did this experience scare you? If yes, it gets filed under "danger" and you're told to avoid anything like it. Did it feel good? Great - now your brain wants to chase that feeling, which can lead you straight into spiritual bypassing or guru worship.

Neither of these responses helps you evaluate what actually happened. They're just your nervous system doing its job. And that's why you can't use logic alone to sort this out. You need something else. You need the part of you that existed before your brain started telling stories.

That part is your intuition.

What Intuition Actually Feels Like (It's Not What You Think)

Most people think intuition is a voice. A little whisper that tells you what to do. And sometimes it is. But more often, intuition doesn't speak in words. It speaks in sensations.

For me, intuition feels like a pressure in my chest. Not pain, not tightness - just a presence. Like someone put a hand there and is gently pushing. When something is true, the pressure releases. When something is false, it stays or gets heavier.

For you, it might be different. Maybe it's a warmth in your belly. Maybe it's a tingling in your hands. Maybe it's a sudden stillness in your mind, like the world just stopped spinning for a second. Maybe it's a feeling of expansion in your heart space, or a contraction in your throat.

The point is: your intuition has a physical signature. And once you learn to recognize it, you can use it to evaluate anything - including your own spiritual experiences.

But here's the catch. Your intuition gets tangled up with your emotions, your trauma, your conditioning, and your wishful thinking. So you can't just ask "did this feel true?" and get a clean answer. You have to learn to separate the signal from the noise.

And that takes practice. Real, messy, human practice.

The Grounding Principle: Before You Evaluate, You Have to Land

Here's a mistake I made for years. I would have a spiritual experience - a vision, a download, a moment of unity with everything - and I would immediately try to figure out what it meant. I'd grab my journal. I'd analyze it. I'd ask my friends what they thought. I'd Google it.

And every time, I ended up more confused than when I started.

Why? Because I was trying to evaluate the experience while I was still in the middle of it. My nervous system was still processing. My energy body was still integrating. I was trying to read the label on a bottle that was still spinning.

The first step in evaluating any spiritual experience is to ground yourself. Fully. Completely. Before you touch it with your mind.

This means getting back into your body. Feel your feet on the floor. Breathe into your belly. Eat something. Drink water. Touch something solid - a wall, a tree, a pet. Let your nervous system settle.

For some people, this takes five minutes. For others, it takes a day or two. There's no rush. The experience isn't going anywhere. It's already inside you. And trying to evaluate it while you're still floating is like trying to read a map in a hurricane.

One of the best tools for this is a simple grounding practice. You can find guided ones in books like See on Amazon (paid link) - look for anything by teachers who emphasize embodiment over transcendence. The goal isn't to leave your body. The goal is to come all the way back into it.

The Three Filters: Time, Body, and Impact

Once you're grounded, you can start evaluating. I use three filters. They're simple, but they've saved me from chasing a lot of nonsense.

Filter One: Time

Does this experience hold up over time? Or does it fade, shift, or lose its power after a few days?

Genuine spiritual openings tend to have a staying power. They don't disappear when the novelty wears off. They might settle into the background, but they leave a residue. A knowing that stays with you even when you're doing laundry or stuck in traffic.

Experiences that are mostly your imagination or your ego tend to fade quickly. They're exciting in the moment, but a week later, you can barely remember what it felt like. They don't change your behavior. They don't alter your perception of reality in any lasting way.

So give it time. Don't make any big decisions based on a spiritual experience for at least a week. Let it sit. Let it marinate. See if it's still there when the initial charge wears off.

Filter Two: Body

When you bring the experience to mind right now - not the memory of it, but the actual felt sense of it - what happens in your body?

Close your eyes. Take a breath. Call up the experience. Not the story about it, but the feeling of it. The energy of it.

Does your body relax? Does your breathing deepen? Do you feel a sense of expansion or openness? Or do you feel tightness, contraction, a closing down?

Your body doesn't lie. It can't. It's not smart enough to lie. It responds to truth with ease and to falsehood with resistance. That's not a metaphor. That's physiology.

If your body relaxes and opens when you bring the experience to mind, pay attention. That's a signal. If your body tightens or closes, that's also a signal. Neither one is definitive on its own, but together they form a pattern.

One caveat: trauma responses can mimic this. If you have a history of spiritual abuse, your body might tighten around anything that feels "spiritual" even if it's genuine. That's not a sign that the experience is false. That's a sign that you have healing to do. Which brings us to the third filter.

Filter Three: Impact

What does this experience lead you toward? Does it make you more loving, more present, more grounded, more compassionate? Or does it make you more confused, more isolated, more superior, more afraid?

This is the test that cuts through almost everything. Genuine spiritual experiences don't make you feel special. They make you feel connected. They don't make you want to tell everyone about how enlightened you are. They make you want to be quiet and serve. They don't inflate your ego. They soften it.

If your experience left you feeling like you're now above other people, or like you have secret knowledge they don't, or like you need to fix everyone around you - that's not a genuine opening. That's your ego co-opting the experience to make itself bigger.

If your experience left you feeling more humble, more tender, more aware of your own smallness in the face of something vast - that's the real thing. That's the signature of something true.

This is where a good book on the subject can help you see patterns you might miss on your own. See on Amazon (paid link) offers a grounded perspective on how to tell the difference between genuine spiritual openings and psychological projections. It's not about dogma. It's about discernment.

What to Do When You're Still Not Sure

Sometimes you go through all three filters and you're still not sure. The experience holds up over time, your body doesn't give you a clear signal, and the impact is mixed. What then?

First, don't force it. Certainty is overrated. You don't need to know right now. You can hold the experience in a container of "I don't know yet" and let it reveal itself over time.

Second, bring it to someone who has more experience than you. Not someone who will tell you what it means, but someone who can hold space for you to discover your own understanding. A good mentor won't give you answers. They'll ask you questions that help you find your own clarity.

Third, test it in your life. Don't just think about the experience. Live it. Act as if it's true and see what happens. If your intuition told you to change careers, don't quit your job tomorrow. But start looking into it. Start taking small steps. See if the path opens or closes in front of you.

This is where intuition becomes a practical tool, not just a woo-woo concept. It's not about getting perfect answers. It's about making better decisions than you could make with logic alone.

If you want to go deeper into how to apply intuition to everyday choices, See on Amazon (paid link) is a solid resource. It's not about spiritual experiences specifically, but it teaches the skill of listening to your inner knowing in a way that translates directly to this work.

The Trap of Spiritual Bypassing

I need to say something blunt. Not every spiritual experience is a healthy experience. Some are trauma responses wearing enlightened robes.

Spiritual bypassing is when you use spiritual beliefs or experiences to avoid dealing with your real human stuff. You have a vision of unity, so you decide you're done with therapy. You feel a rush of cosmic love, so you ignore the fact that your relationship is falling apart. You get a download about forgiveness, so you pretend that person who hurt you doesn't need to be held accountable.

That's not spirituality. That's avoidance. And it will eventually collapse under its own weight.

Your intuition can help you spot this. If a spiritual experience makes you want to skip the hard work of being human - the therapy, the boundary-setting, the grief, the anger, the mess - then something is off. Genuine spiritual openings don't bypass your humanity. They deepen it. They make you more able to be with your own pain, not less.

If you find yourself using spiritual experiences to escape, that's a red flag. Step back. Get grounded. And maybe find a therapist who understands this stuff.

When Your Intuition Says No But Your Heart Says Yes

This is the hardest one. You've had an experience that felt beautiful. It filled you with hope. It gave you a sense of purpose. You want it to be real so badly you can taste it.

But something in your gut is saying "wait." Something is holding back. There's a quiet "no" that you keep trying to talk over.

Listen to that "no." Even if it breaks your heart. Even if it means letting go of something that felt like salvation.

Your intuition's job isn't to make you feel good. It's to keep you safe and aligned. And sometimes "safe and aligned" means walking away from something that feels amazing but isn't actually true for you.

I've been there. I once had an experience that I was sure was a calling from a specific spiritual tradition. It felt electric. It felt destined. But my intuition kept whispering "this isn't yours." It took me two years to finally listen. And when I did, I didn't lose anything. I found a path that was actually mine, not one I borrowed from someone else's experience.

This is where a book like See on Amazon (paid link) can help. It's about the courage to trust yourself even when the world tells you not to. And that's exactly what you're doing when you evaluate a spiritual experience honestly.

The Only Question That Really Matters

At the end of all the filters, all the practices, all the books, there's one question that cuts through everything:

Does this experience help you live more fully as a human being?

Not as a spiritual being having a human experience. That's true, but it's also a bypass. The real question is: does this help you be more present in your actual life? Does it help you love the people in front of you? Does it help you do your work with more care? Does it help you face your own shadow with more compassion?

If the answer is yes, then it doesn't matter whether the experience was "real" in some objective sense. It's real in the only way that matters: it's real in its effects. It's real in how it changes you.

If the answer is no, then it also doesn't matter whether it was objectively real. It's not serving you. And you can let it go.

Your intuition already knows this. You don't need to figure out the metaphysics. You just need to ask the question and feel the answer in your body.

Does this help me live more fully? Yes or no.

That's it. That's the whole practice.

You Get to Trust Yourself

I know you've been told otherwise. I know you've been told you're too sensitive, too imaginative, too emotional. I know you've been told to doubt yourself, to check with authority figures, to run everything through a rational filter before you're allowed to believe it.

But here's the truth: you are the only one who can evaluate your own experience. No guru, no book, no teacher, no system can do it for you. They can give you tools. They can offer perspectives. But the final discernment is yours and yours alone.

And you are capable of it. Your intuition is not broken. It's just been buried under years of conditioning. But it's still there. It's still working. It's still trying to get your attention.

So start small. Take one experience - one that's been sitting in your mind, unresolved - and run it through the filters. Give it time. Check your body. Look at the impact. See what comes up.

And trust what you find. Not blindly. Not without questioning. But with the quiet confidence that comes from knowing your own inner compass works.

Because it does. It always has. You just needed permission to use it.

Ready to go deeper? Take the free assessment at intuitivesawaken.com/assessment to discover your intuitive type and get a personalized practice for building discernment. It's free, it takes about 10 minutes, and it will show you exactly where your intuition is already strong and where it needs a little more support.
